title: Alternating knots with unknotting number one
overall_summary: McCoy proves that for an alternating knot, unknotting number one is equivalent to its branched double cover arising by half-integer surgery on a knot in S^3, and also equivalent to the existence of an unknotting crossing in any reduced alternating diagram. The proof is driven by lattice theory: Goeritz lattices of reduced alternating diagrams are treated as graph lattices, then compared with Greene-style change-maker lattices obtained from Heegaard Floer and Donaldson-theoretic obstructions. For the original problem, the most useful content is Section 2, which develops irreducibility in positive-definite integral lattices, especially a graph-lattice characterization of irreducible vectors and methods for certifying irreducibility using decompositions and sign/support arguments. Later parts of the paper also provide reusable estimates: if a graph lattice is embedded in a change-maker lattice, sums of vertices have tightly bounded coordinates because any attempted decomposition has non-positive cross-term.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition in Section 2: irreducible element and indecomposable lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A positive-definite integral lattice L is a finitely generated free abelian group with a positive-definite, symmetric, integer-valued bilinear form, written x¬∑y. Its norm is ‚à•v‚à•^2=v¬∑v. The lattice L is indecomposable if it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum L=L_1‚äïL_2 with L_1 and L_2 nonzero sublattices. An element v‚ààL is irreducible if there do not exist nonzero x,y‚ààL such that v=x+y and x¬∑y‚â•0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is definitional in the paper. The exact irreducibility condition is used throughout: to prove a vector is irreducible one rules out decompositions with nonnegative cross-term, and to exploit irreducibility one produces a natural decomposition v=a+b and shows a¬∑b‚â•0, forcing one summand to vanish.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 together with equations (2.1) and (2.2): non-positive cross-term for sums of vertices in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, allowing multiple edges. For vertices u,v let e(u,v) be the number of edges between them, and let d(v)=‚àë_{u‚ààV} e(u,v). Let Œõ(G) be the quotient of the free abelian group on V by the subgroup generated by [V]=‚àë_{v‚ààV}v, equipped with the form v¬∑v=d(v) and u¬∑v=‚àíe(u,v) for u‚âÝv. For R‚äÜV write [R]=‚àë_{v‚ààR}v. If x=[R] is a sum of vertices and z‚ààŒõ(G) is arbitrary, then (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0. More explicitly, if z is represented by ‚àë_{v‚ààV} b_v v and b_{v,u}=b_v‚àíb_u, then ([R]‚àíz)¬∑z=‚àë_{u‚ààR,v‚ààV‚àñR} b_{v,u}(1‚àíb_{v,u})e(v,u) ‚àí (1/2)‚àë_{u,v‚ààR} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u) ‚àí (1/2)‚àë_{u,v‚ààV‚àñR} b_{v,u}^2 e(v,u), and every summand on the right is non-positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For y=‚àë c_v v one first computes v¬∑y=‚àë_{u‚ààV}(c_v‚àíc_u)e(v,u). Multiplying by the coefficients b_v of x=‚àë b_v v and symmetrizing gives x¬∑y=(1/2)‚àë_{u,v‚ààV}(c_v‚àíc_u)(b_v‚àíb_u)e(v,u). Put y=[R]‚àíz, so c_v=1‚àíb_v for v‚ààR and c_v=‚àíb_v for v‚àâR. Substitution yields the displayed formula. Since b_{v,u} is an integer, b_{v,u}(1‚àíb_{v,u})‚â§0, and the square terms are also non-positive; hence ([R]‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2: irreducible vectors in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, and let Œõ(G) be its graph lattice as in Lemma 2.1. For a nonzero element x‚ààŒõ(G), x is irreducible, meaning x cannot be written as a+b with nonzero a,b and a¬∑b‚â•0, if and only if x=[R] for some subset R‚äÜV such that both induced subgraphs G[R] and G[V‚àñR] are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction, write x=‚àë a_v v and use the relation [V]=0 to choose the integer coefficients with min_v a_v=0. Let a=max_v a_v‚â•1 and let R={v:a_v=a}. A direct calculation of (x‚àí[R])¬∑[R] shows that internal edges of R cancel and each edge from R to V‚àñR contributes (a‚àí1)‚àía_u‚â•0, because a_u‚â§a‚àí1 outside R. Thus (x‚àí[R])¬∑[R]‚â•0. Since [R] is nonzero and x is irreducible, the decomposition x=[R]+(x‚àí[R]) forces x=[R]. If G[R] were disconnected, say R=S‚à™T with e(S,T)=0, then [R]=[S]+[T] and [S]¬∑[T]=0 would contradict irreducibility; applying the same argument to ‚àíx=[V‚àñR] shows G[V‚àñR] is connected. Conversely, assume x=[R] and both R and V‚àñR induce connected subgraphs. If [R]=y+([R]‚àíy) with ([R]‚àíy)¬∑y‚â•0, Lemma 2.1 gives equality. In the formula from Lemma 2.1, equality forces all square terms to vanish, so the coefficients of y are constant on R and constant on V‚àñR. The edge terms across the cut force the two constants to differ by either 0 or 1. Therefore y is either 0 or [R] in the quotient, so no nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative cross-term exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3: 2-connected graphs, irreducible vertices, and indecomposable graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, and let Œõ(G) be its graph lattice. The following are equivalent: (i) G is 2-connected, i.e. deleting any vertex does not disconnect G; (ii) every vertex v‚ààV, viewed as an element of Œõ(G), is irreducible; (iii) Œõ(G) is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nonzero sublattices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (i) and (ii) is Lemma 2.2 applied to R={v}: the singleton is connected, and the complement is connected exactly when v is not a cut vertex. For (ii)‚áí(iii), suppose Œõ(G)=L_1‚äïL_2. Since each vertex is irreducible, its two orthogonal projections cannot both be nonzero, so every vertex lies entirely in L_1 or L_2. Let R_i be the vertices lying in L_i. Then [R_1]+[R_2]=[V]=0 and [R_1]¬∑[R_2]=0, hence [R_1]^2=0. Positive-definiteness forces [R_1]=0, and connectedness implies the partition is trivial; if L_1 is nonzero, all vertices lie in L_1 and L_2=0. For (iii)‚áí(i), if G has a cut vertex v, then G‚àñ{v} has at least two nonempty components; grouping these into two nonempty parts gives two nonzero orthogonal sublattices whose direct sum is Œõ(G), contradicting indecomposability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Implicit corollary used in Lemma 2.4: norm-one vectors and cut-edges in graph lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no self-loops and graph lattice Œõ(G). If G has no cut-edge, then Œõ(G) contains no vector of norm 1. More precisely, a cut-edge separating a subset R from V‚àñR gives [R]^2=1, and conversely a norm-one vector in Œõ(G) would correspond, via Lemma 2.2, to a subset R with boundary size d(R)=1, hence to a cut-edge.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity [R]^2=d(R), where d(R)=e(R,V‚àñR), follows directly from the graph-lattice form or from the edge-energy formula. A vector of norm 1 in a positive-definite integral lattice is automatically irreducible: if z=a+b with a,b nonzero and a¬∑b‚â•0, then 1=z^2=a^2+b^2+2a¬∑b‚â•2. Lemma 2.2 then gives z=[R] with both sides connected, and z^2=d(R)=1. A boundary of size one is exactly a cut-edge.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4: a decomposition v=x+y with x¬∑y=‚àí1 detects a cut-edge after deleting v
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a finite connected graph with no self-loops, assume G is 2-connected and contains no cut-edges, and let Œõ(G) be its graph lattice. Suppose a vertex v‚ààV admits a decomposition v=x+y in Œõ(G) with x,y‚ààŒõ(G) and x¬∑y=‚àí1. Then G‚àñ{v} contains a cut-edge e. If R and S are the vertex sets of the two components of (G‚àñ{v})‚àñ{e}, then {x,y}={[R]+v,[S]+v}. Moreover, if u_1‚ààR and u_2‚ààS are the endpoints of e, then they are the unique vertices different from v satisfying x¬∑u_1=1 and y¬∑u_2=1; every vertex w‚àâ{v,u_1,u_2} satisfies w¬∑x‚â§0 and w¬∑y‚â§0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First prove x and y are irreducible. If x=z+w with z¬∑w‚â•0, then because x¬∑y=‚àí1, at least one of z¬∑y or w¬∑y is nonnegative; assume w¬∑y‚â•0. Then w¬∑(y+z)‚â•0, and v=w+(y+z). Since G is 2-connected, v is irreducible by Lemma 2.3, so either w=0 or y+z=0. In the latter case w=v and y=‚àíz; then x¬∑y=‚àí1 and z¬∑w‚â•0 imply z¬∑(v+z)=1 with v¬∑z‚â•0, hence ‚à•z‚à•^2‚â§1. As G has no cut-edges, Œõ(G) has no norm-one vectors, so z=0. Thus x is irreducible, and similarly y is. By Lemma 2.2, x=[R‚Ä≤] and y=[S‚Ä≤] for subsets with connected induced sides. The equality v=[R‚Ä≤]+[S‚Ä≤] in the quotient forces R‚Ä≤‚à™S‚Ä≤=V and R‚Ä≤‚à©S‚Ä≤={v}; write R=R‚Ä≤‚àñ{v} and S=S‚Ä≤‚àñ{v}. Thus x=v+[R] and y=v+[S], with R‚à™S=V‚àñ{v}. The irreducibility conditions imply R, S, R‚à™{v}, and S‚à™{v} induce connected subgraphs. Since [R]+[S]=‚àív, one computes x¬∑y=[R]¬∑[S]=‚àíe(R,S)=‚àí1, so there is exactly one edge between R and S. That edge is a cut-edge of G‚àñ{v}; the final uniqueness and sign assertions follow by computing pairings of x and y with vertices in R and S, where only the endpoints of the unique R‚ÄìS edge pair positively.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5: Brown's subset-sum characterization of change-maker sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let œÉ_1‚â§œÉ_2‚â§‚ãØ‚â§œÉ_s be nonnegative integers. Every integer k with 0‚â§k‚â§œÉ_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_s can be expressed as k=‚àë_{i‚ààA}œÉ_i for some subset A‚äÜ{1,‚Ä¶,s} if and only if the sequence satisfies the change-maker inequalities œÉ_1‚â§1 and œÉ_i‚â§œÉ_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_{i‚àí1}+1 for every 1<i‚â§s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as a theorem of J. L. Brown and does not reprove it. McCoy uses it as the combinatorial engine allowing one to manufacture lattice vectors with prescribed œÉ-pairing zero: an integer bounded by a partial sum of the œÉ_i can be represented as a subset sum of earlier coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6: representing œÉ_s by earlier coefficients, with control of the index 0
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=‚ü®œÅ,œÉ‚ü©^‚ä•‚äÜ‚Ñ§^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice, where œÅ=e_{‚àí1}‚àíe_0 and œÉ=e_0+œÉ_1e_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_re_r, and assume œÉ_i‚â•1 for i‚â•1, so œÉ_1=1. Write œÉ_0=1 for the coefficient of e_0. For any s>1, there is a subset A‚äÜ{0,1,‚Ä¶,s‚àí1} such that 1‚ààA and œÉ_s=‚àë_{i‚ààA}œÉ_i. If the sequence œÉ is slack, meaning no index t>1 satisfies œÉ_t=1+œÉ_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_{t‚àí1}, then A may be chosen with 0‚àâA.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states that this follows by induction on s. One inductive reconstruction is as follows. If œÉ_s is tight at s, take A={0,1,‚Ä¶,s‚àí1}. If œÉ_s is not tight, the change-maker inequality gives œÉ_s‚â§œÉ_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_{s‚àí1}, so Brown's proposition gives a subset B‚äÜ{1,‚Ä¶,s‚àí1} summing to œÉ_s. If 1‚ààB, take A=B, which already avoids 0. If 1‚àâB, let j be the smallest element of B. By induction, œÉ_j can be expressed as a sum over A_j‚äÜ{0,‚Ä¶,j‚àí1} containing 1; replacing j in B by A_j produces a subset summing to œÉ_s, and minimality of j avoids overlap with B‚àñ{j}. If the whole sequence is slack, every initial segment is slack, so the inductive representation of œÉ_j can be chosen without 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7: standard vectors form a basis of a change-maker lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=‚ü®œÅ,œÉ‚ü©^‚ä•‚äÜ‚Ñ§^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with œÅ=e_{‚àí1}‚àíe_0 and œÉ=e_0+œÉ_1e_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_re_r, and assume œÉ_i‚â•1 for i‚â•1. For each s=1,‚Ä¶,r define a standard vector v_s as follows. If œÉ_s=1+œÉ_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_{s‚àí1}, set v_s=‚àíe_s+e_{‚àí1}+e_0+e_1+‚ãØ+e_{s‚àí1}. Otherwise choose A_s‚äÜ{1,‚Ä¶,s‚àí2} with œÉ_s‚àíœÉ_{s‚àí1}=‚àë_{i‚ààA_s}œÉ_i and set v_s=‚àíe_s+e_{s‚àí1}+‚àë_{i‚ààA_s}e_i. Then S={v_1,‚Ä¶,v_r} is a basis for L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vectors are triangular with respect to the ordered basis e_1,‚Ä¶,e_r: v_k has coefficient ‚àí1 on e_k, and no v_j with j<k has e_k in its support. Hence they are linearly independent. To show they span, take any v‚ààL and let k=max supp(v) among indices ‚â•1. Adding (v¬∑e_k)v_k kills the e_k coefficient and does not create any larger indexed coefficient. Iterating reduces to a vector whose support is contained in {‚àí1,0}. Orthogonality to œÅ then makes it a(e_{‚àí1}+e_0), and orthogonality to œÉ forces a=0. Thus every element of L is an integer combination of the standard vectors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.9: irreducible vectors in change-maker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=‚ü®œÅ,œÉ‚ü©^‚ä•‚äÜ‚Ñ§^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with œÅ=e_{‚àí1}‚àíe_0 and œÉ=e_0+œÉ_1e_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_re_r, and assume œÉ_i‚â•1 for all i‚â•1. If v=‚àíe_k+‚àë_{i‚ààA}e_i lies in L with A‚äÇ{‚àí1,0,1,‚Ä¶,k‚àí1}, then v is irreducible in L. In particular, every standard basis element from Lemma 2.7 is irreducible. Additionally, if œÉ_k is tight, then the vector v=‚àíe_k+e_{k‚àí1}+‚ãØ+e_2+2e_1 is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First observe that any nonzero y‚ààL has both positive and negative support: orthogonality to œÅ ties the e_{‚àí1} and e_0 coefficients, and since œÉ has positive coefficients on e_0,e_1,‚Ä¶,e_r, a vector with all coefficients of one sign cannot be orthogonal to œÉ unless it is zero. For v=‚àíe_k+‚àë_{i‚ààA}e_i, suppose v=x+y with x,y‚ààL and x¬∑y‚â•0. In each coordinate, x_i+y_i is ‚àí1, 0, or 1, so x_i y_i‚â§0. Thus x¬∑y=‚àë x_i y_i‚â§0, and the assumed inequality forces every coordinate product to be zero. At the k-coordinate, one summand has coefficient ‚àí1; assume it is x. Then y has no negative coefficient anywhere, so y=0 by the sign-support observation. This proves irreducibility of all such 0/1-support vectors. For the additional tight vector, write y_i=v_i‚àíx_i and compute x¬∑y=‚àíx_{‚àí1}^2‚àíx_0^2+(2‚àíx_1)x_1+‚àë_{i=2}^{k‚àí1}(1‚àíx_i)x_i‚àí(x_k+1)x_k‚àí‚àë_{i=k+1}^r x_i^2. All terms except possibly (2‚àíx_1)x_1 are non-positive, and that term is at most 1. Hence x¬∑y‚â•0 implies x_1y_1 is 0 or 1. If x_1y_1=0, all coordinate products vanish and one summand has no negative support, so it is zero. If x_1y_1=1, then x_1=y_1=1 and there can be at most one coordinate with negative product. Since both nonzero summands must have negative support, after swapping summands one has supp‚àí(y)={k} and supp‚àí(x)={l} with l>k, while supp+(x)‚äÜ{1,‚Ä¶,k‚àí1}. Orthogonality œÉ¬∑x=0 would express œÉ_l as a sum of coefficients strictly before k, which is less than œÉ_k because œÉ_k is tight; this contradicts monotonicity œÉ_l‚â•œÉ_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.10: indecomposable change-maker lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L=‚ü®œÅ,œÉ‚ü©^‚ä•‚äÜ‚Ñ§^{r+2} be a change-maker lattice with œÅ=e_{‚àí1}‚àíe_0 and œÉ=e_0+œÉ_1e_1+‚ãØ+œÉ_re_r. The following are equivalent: (i) L is indecomposable; (ii) œÉ_s‚â•1 for every s‚â•1; (iii) L contains no vector of norm 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If some œÉ_s=0, then e_s lies in L and gives a norm-one summand, so L is decomposable and contains a norm-one vector. Conversely, because the ambient lattice has orthonormal basis, any norm-one vector in L must be ¬±e_i; the orthogonality conditions allow this only for an index i‚â•1 with œÉ_i=0. This proves the equivalence of (ii) and (iii) and the implication (i)‚áí(ii). For (ii)‚áí(i), take the standard basis v_1,‚Ä¶,v_r. By Lemma 2.9 all v_i are irreducible. If L=L_1‚äïL_2, each v_i lies entirely in one summand. Suppose v_1‚ààL_1 and choose the least k with v_k‚ààL_2, if any exists. Since œÉ_k>0, the standard vector v_k has a smallest support index m<k. If m>0, then v_k¬∑v_m=‚àí1; if m‚â§0, then m=‚àí1 and v_k¬∑v_1=1. Either case contradicts orthogonality of L_1 and L_2. Thus no such k exists and the decomposition is trivial.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: a change-maker test vector gives cross-term ‚àí1 or 0
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Section 5, let D be a reduced alternating diagram whose Goeritz lattice Œõ_D is identified with a change-maker lattice L=‚ü®œÅ,œÉ‚ü©^‚ä•‚äÜ‚Ñ§^{r+2}; let V_D be the images in L of the vertices of the corresponding white graph. If x is either a single element of V_D or a sum of elements of V_D, and if z=‚àíe_g+‚àë_{i‚ààA}e_i lies in L with A‚äÜsupp+(x):={i:x¬∑e_i>0} and g‚àâsupp+(x), then (x‚àíz)¬∑z is either ‚àí1 or 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because x is a vertex or a sum of vertices in a graph lattice, Lemma 2.1 gives (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0. Directly in the ambient orthonormal lattice, (x‚àíz)¬∑z=‚àíx¬∑e_g‚àí1+‚àë_{i‚ààA}(x¬∑e_i‚àí1). The hypotheses give x¬∑e_g‚â§0 and x¬∑e_i‚â•1 for i‚ààA, so the right-hand side is at least ‚àí1. It is integral, hence it must be ‚àí1 or 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3: coefficient bounds for sums of vertices in a graph lattice embedded as a change-maker lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same Section 5 setting, let x be a single vertex of V_D or a sum of vertices of V_D. Then: (i) |x¬∑e_0|‚â§1; (ii) |x¬∑e_1|‚â§2; (iii) if x is irreducible and x¬∑e_0‚âÝ0, then |x¬∑e_1|‚â§1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the total sum of all graph vertices is zero in the graph lattice, ‚àíx is again a sum of vertices, so signs can be chosen conveniently. For (i), assume x¬∑e_0‚â•0. Choose the least g>0 with x¬∑e_g‚â§0. By the change-maker condition, write œÉ_g‚àí1 as a subset sum of earlier œÉ_i and form z=‚àíe_g+e_0+e_{‚àí1}+‚àë_{i‚ààA}e_i‚ààL. Lemma 2.1 gives (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0, while minimality of g and x¬∑e_0=x¬∑e_{‚àí1} give the lower bound (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â•‚àí1‚àíx¬∑e_g+(x¬∑e_0‚àí1)+(x¬∑e_{‚àí1}‚àí1)‚â•2x¬∑e_0‚àí3. Thus x¬∑e_0‚â§3/2, hence x¬∑e_0‚â§1. Applying the same argument to ‚àíx gives |x¬∑e_0|‚â§1. For (ii), assume x¬∑e_1‚â•0 and choose the least g>1 with x¬∑e_g‚â§0. Build z=‚àíe_g+e_1+‚àë_{i‚ààA}e_i‚ààL with z¬∑e_1=Œµ‚àà{1,2}. Lemma 2.1 yields 0‚â•(x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â•‚àí1+Œµ(x¬∑e_1‚àíŒµ), so x¬∑e_1‚â§Œµ+1/Œµ‚â§5/2 and integrality gives x¬∑e_1‚â§2; again apply this to ‚àíx. For (iii), assume x is irreducible and x¬∑e_0=1. Let g>0 be least with x¬∑e_g‚â§0. If g=1, take z=‚àíe_1+e_0+e_{‚àí1}, which is irreducible by Lemma 2.9. If x‚âÝz, irreducibility of x forces (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§‚àí1, and the computation (x‚àíz)¬∑z=‚àí(x¬∑e_1+1), together with minimality, gives ‚àí1‚â§x¬∑e_1‚â§0; if x=z then x¬∑e_1=‚àí1. If g>1, choose z so that z¬∑e_1=1, using either e_0+e_{‚àí1} or e_1 according to whether 1 belongs to the subset-sum representation. If x‚âÝz, irreducibility gives (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§‚àí1, while the same coordinate estimate gives x¬∑e_1‚â§1; minimality gives x¬∑e_1‚â•1. Thus x¬∑e_1=1. The case x¬∑e_0=‚àí1 follows by replacing x with ‚àíx.

proof_techniques.name: Edge-wise energy expansion for graph lattices
proof_techniques.description: The paper rewrites the graph-lattice pairing as a sum over ordered pairs of vertices, equivalently as an edge-energy expression involving coefficient differences. When one summand is [R], the expression for ([R]‚àíz)¬∑z splits into contributions from edges inside R, inside V‚àñR, and across the cut; each contribution is non-positive for integral coefficients. This converts an algebraic condition about decompositions into local inequalities along edges.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree lattice, an analogous expansion for a proposed decomposition of a vertex or of a connected subtree sum may expose sign restrictions branch-by-branch. The key transferable idea is to express cross-terms using coefficient differences and then force each local term to have a sign.

proof_techniques.name: Extremal coefficient or top-level-set extraction
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Lemma 2.2, an arbitrary lattice element x is represented with minimum coefficient 0, and the subset R on which the coefficient is maximal is isolated. The decomposition x=[R]+(x‚àí[R]) has nonnegative cross-term, so irreducibility of x forces x=[R]. Connectivity of R and its complement is then forced by excluding decompositions [R]=[S]+[T] with zero cross-term.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex in the weighted tree is assumed reducible or if one studies a minimal counterexample, choosing coefficient maxima/minima in a decomposition may identify a connected branch or level set whose pairing has controlled sign. This can turn irreducibility questions into combinatorics of connected subtrees.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-theoretic characterization of irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: For graph lattices, McCoy proves that irreducible elements are exactly the sums [R] where both R and V‚àñR induce connected subgraphs. As a special case, every graph vertex is irreducible exactly when the graph has no cut vertex, and graph-lattice indecomposability is equivalent to 2-connectedness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original lattice is a weighted tree lattice rather than a graph Laplacian quotient, this suggests looking for a graph-theoretic meaning of a reducible vertex: reducibility should correspond to splitting the tree into branches with controlled interaction. The theorem provides a model for translating algebraic irreducibility into connectivity.

proof_techniques.name: Norm-one obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the fact that graph lattices with no cut-edges have no norm-one vectors, since a norm-one vector would be irreducible and hence a cut [R] with exactly one boundary edge. More generally, in any positive-definite integral lattice a norm-one vector cannot decompose as a+b with a,b nonzero and a¬∑b‚â•0 because the norm would be at least 2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree has any vertex of weight 1, that vertex is immediately irreducible by the same norm-one argument. More broadly, ruling out or locating small-norm vectors can sharply constrain possible decompositions of vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Reducible vertex localizes to a cut
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.4 analyzes a decomposition v=x+y with x¬∑y=‚àí1 in a graph lattice. Under 2-connectedness and absence of cut-edges, it first proves x and y are themselves irreducible, then uses the classification of irreducibles to show that x and y are sums over the two sides of a unique edge in G‚àñ{v}. Thus an algebraic decomposition of one vertex identifies a very specific graph cut.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a tree lattice, every vertex removal already separates branches. A parallel argument might show that any reducible decomposition of a vertex must align with a partition of incident branches; this could be used to find a vertex whose decomposition is impossible under positive-definiteness and the bad-weight hypothesis.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducibility from sparse support in an orthonormal ambient lattice
proof_techniques.description: For change-maker lattices inside ‚Ñ§^{r+2}, McCoy proves that vectors of the form ‚àíe_k plus a 0/1 sum of earlier basis vectors are irreducible. The proof is coordinate-wise: if v=x+y and x¬∑y‚â•0, then every coordinate product x_i y_i is non-positive, so all must vanish; one summand is then forced to have no negative coordinate, impossible for a nonzero vector in the change-maker lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted tree lattice can be embedded into a diagonal lattice, or if candidate vertices can be expressed in a similarly sparse coordinate form, this gives a direct certificate of irreducibility that avoids global case analysis.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducible generating set plus nonzero-pairing graph implies indecomposability
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 2.10, standard basis vectors of a change-maker lattice are irreducible. If the lattice decomposed orthogonally, each irreducible basis vector would lie in one summand; the triangular support structure guarantees nonzero pairings linking each new basis element to an earlier one, forcing all basis elements into the same summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted tree, if one can construct enough irreducible elements supported near vertices or branches, nonzero adjacency pairings may propagate them through the connected tree. This can rule out orthogonal decompositions or help isolate where irreducible vertices must occur.

proof_techniques.name: Constructing test vectors from subset-sum conditions
proof_techniques.description: The change-maker condition, via Brown's theorem and Lemma 2.6, lets the paper build lattice vectors z with a prescribed negative coordinate and positive coordinates chosen from a desired support. These z are then inserted into the graph-lattice inequality (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0 to force sharp coefficient bounds, often narrowing a cross-term to only ‚àí1 or 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem may admit analogous test vectors built from paths or branches in the tree. If such vectors can be chosen to match the sign pattern of a suspected decomposition, the same strategy can turn positivity into rigid coefficient bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Equality-case analysis in non-positive decompositions
proof_techniques.description: Several arguments use an inequality such as (x‚àíz)¬∑z‚â§0 and then force equality by an independent lower bound or irreducibility. Equality in the edge-wise formula is very strong: coefficient differences must vanish along internal edges, and cross-edge differences must be 0 or 1. This converts a global algebraic equality into exact local coefficient constraints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When positive-definiteness or minimality forces a cross-term to be nonnegative while a structural inequality forces it to be nonpositive, the equality case may determine the coefficients of a decomposition on every branch of the tree.

other_useful_info: Section 2.1 is explicitly based on Greene, The lens space realization problem, Section 3.2. Change-maker lattices are drawn from Greene's work on lens spaces, cabling, and unknotting number one. The paper repeatedly uses the identities [R]^2=d(R) in a graph lattice and [V]=0 in the quotient, and also uses that if x is irreducible then ‚àíx is irreducible. The later knot-theoretic parts identify Goeritz lattices of reduced alternating diagrams with graph lattices and then with change-maker lattices; those topological applications are probably less directly relevant than the lattice lemmas themselves for the original weighted-tree irreducibility problem.
why_relevant: Uses the exact irreducibility definition from the problem for positive-definite integral lattices. Its graph-lattice and changemaker-lattice lemmas give concrete techniques for proving that specified vertex-like lattice elements are irreducible.
